title: Kubernetes Pod Pending from kube-scheduler FailedScheduling (Placement and Fit)
exclusions: Does not replace UC-3.2.5 PersistentVolumeClaim provisioning or bind failures. Does not replace UC-3.2.46 Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter scale-out refusal narratives. Does not replace UC-3.2.4 namespace ResourceQuota admission exhaustion. Does not replace UC-3.2.10 CrashLoopBackOff. Does not detect ImagePullBackOff or container create errors without scheduler placement evidence. Does not automatically change Pod specs; it signals human-approved remediation.
evidence: Saved search uc_3_2_2_scheduler_failed_scheduling with five-minute schedule; lookups/critical_namespaces.csv versioned in git; weekly CSV export of alert rows to a restricted evidence index with lookup commit hash for auditors.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In lab namespace qa-sched-lab, create a Deployment whose Pods request CPU greater than any node allocatable in that pool; confirm kube:events shows FailedScheduling, kube_pod_status_phase pending persists, and uc_3_2_2_scheduler_failed_scheduling returns a row with severity at least medium after the soak gate.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Lower requests to fit allocatable or add a suitable node, wait for phase Running and scheduled condition true in metrics, confirm FailedScheduling stops, and verify the saved search emits no qualifying row for that pod.

dataSources: index=k8s_metrics sourcetype=prometheus:scrape:metrics from kube-state-metrics exposing kube_pod_status_phase, kube_pod_status_scheduled, and from kube-scheduler or aggregated scrapes exposing scheduler_pending_pods, scheduler_unschedulable_pods, scheduler_schedule_attempts_total; index=k8s or index=k8s_events sourcetype=kube:events for reason FailedScheduling on Pod involved objects; governance join via lookup critical_namespaces.csv for workload_tier and owner_team.
app: Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Kubernetes ([Splunk Add-on for Kubernetes documentation](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/AddOns/released/Kubernetes/About)) with prometheus receiver scraping kube-state-metrics and kube-scheduler metrics where permitted, k8s_events receiver for v1.Event streams, splunk_hec exporters; kube-prometheus-stack ServiceMonitor acceptable when label contracts match the SPL coalesce ladders; Splunk Enterprise 9.2+ or Splunk Cloud search tier for scheduled alerts.
spl:
`comment("UC-3.2.2 K8s kube-scheduler FailedScheduling axis. idx=k8s_metrics,k8s earliest=-45m@m latest=now pending_soak_alert_min=8")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, aks_cluster_name, resource_cluster, ""))))
      | eval mn=trim(toString(coalesce(__name__, metric_name, name, "")))
      | eval mv=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(value, metric_value, Value, "")), 10)
      | eval ph=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(phase, phase_name, ""))))
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(coalesce(namespace, k8s_namespace, kube_namespace, ns, "")))
      | eval pod=trim(toString(coalesce(pod, pod_name, "")))
      | where mn="kube_pod_status_phase" AND ph="pending" AND mv>0 AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(namespace)>0 AND len(pod)>0
      | bucket _time span=1m
      | stats max(mv) AS pend BY cluster namespace pod _time
      | where pend>0
      | sort 0 cluster namespace pod + _time
      | streamstats current=t global=f first(_time) AS pend_start BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval pending_soak_min=round((now()-pend_start)/60, 3)
      | stats latest(pending_soak_min) AS pending_soak_min BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval sched_lane="phase_pending"
      | eval fs_message=""
      | eval fs_event_time=0 ]
    [ search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, aks_cluster_name, resource_cluster, ""))))
      | eval mn=trim(toString(coalesce(__name__, metric_name, name, "")))
      | eval mv=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(value, metric_value, Value, "")), 10)
      | eval cond=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(condition, ""))))
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(coalesce(namespace, k8s_namespace, kube_namespace, ns, "")))
      | eval pod=trim(toString(coalesce(pod, pod_name, "")))
      | where mn="kube_pod_status_scheduled" AND cond="false" AND mv>0 AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(namespace)>0 AND len(pod)>0
      | stats latest(mv) AS unsched_metric BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval pending_soak_min=0
      | eval sched_lane="scheduled_false"
      | eval fs_message=""
      | eval fs_event_time=0 ]
    [ search (index=k8s OR index=k8s_events) sourcetype="kube:events" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, source_cluster, ""))))
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(coalesce(metadata_namespace, namespace, involvedObject_namespace, "")))
      | eval pod=trim(toString(coalesce(involvedObject_name, `involvedObject.name`, "")))
      | eval ik=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(involvedObject_kind, `involvedObject.kind`, ""))))
      | eval reason=trim(toString(coalesce(reason, Reason, "")))
      | eval msg=toString(coalesce(message, Message, ""))
      | where reason="FailedScheduling" AND ik="pod" AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(namespace)>0 AND len(pod)>0
      | stats latest(msg) AS fs_message max(_time) AS fs_event_time BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval pending_soak_min=0
      | eval sched_lane="kube_event" ]
| stats max(pending_soak_min) AS pending_soak_min values(sched_lane) AS lane_mv max(fs_event_time) AS fs_event_time values(fs_message) AS fs_msg_mv BY cluster namespace pod
| eval fs_message=coalesce(nullif(trim(mvindex(mvdedup(fs_msg_mv),0)),""), "")
| eval sched_lane=mvjoin(mvdedup(lane_mv), "|")
| eventstats max(pending_soak_min) AS cluster_peak_pending BY cluster
| join type=left max=0 cluster
    [| search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, aks_cluster_name, resource_cluster, ""))))
      | eval mn=trim(toString(coalesce(__name__, metric_name, name, "")))
      | eval mv=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(value, metric_value, Value, "")), 10)
      | where mn IN ("scheduler_pending_pods","scheduler_unschedulable_pods","scheduler_schedule_attempts_total")
      | eval sp=if(mn=="scheduler_pending_pods",mv,null())
      | eval su=if(mn=="scheduler_unschedulable_pods",mv,null())
      | eval sat=if(mn=="scheduler_schedule_attempts_total",mv,null())
      | stats latest(sp) AS scheduler_pending_pods latest(su) AS scheduler_unschedulable_pods latest(sat) AS scheduler_schedule_attempts_total BY cluster ]
| join type=left max=0 namespace
    [| inputlookup critical_namespaces.csv
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(namespace))
      | eval workload_tier=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(workload_tier, env_tier, tier, criticality, "standard"))))
      | eval owner_team=trim(toString(coalesce(owner_team, squad, on_call_team, "")))
      | fields namespace workload_tier owner_team ]
| fillnull value=0 pending_soak_min cluster_peak_pending scheduler_pending_pods scheduler_unschedulable_pods scheduler_schedule_attempts_total fs_event_time
| fillnull value="" fs_message sched_lane
| eval failure_class=case(
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient cpu"), "insufficient_cpu",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient memory"), "insufficient_memory",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient ephemeral-storage|insufficient ephemeral storage"), "insufficient_ephemeral_storage",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient.*pods|too many pods|exceeded maxpod"), "pod_count_exhaustion",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)didn\'t match pod\'s node affinity|didn\'t match node selector|node(s)? didn\'t match pod\'s node affinity"), "node_affinity_or_selector",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)had untolerated taint|not tolerate|untolerated taint"), "taints_without_tolerations",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)topology spread constraint|topologyspreadconstraints"), "topology_spread_unsatisfiable",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)0/\\d+ nodes are available"), "no_nodes_available_summary",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)match pod affinity|match inter-pod affinity|pod affinity"), "pod_affinity_anti_affinity",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)nvidia\\.com/gpu|amd\\.com/gpu|gpu"), "extended_resource_gpu",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)node(s)? had volume node affinity conflict|required node missing|local persistent volume"), "local_pv_or_volume_node_affinity",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)node(s)? had disk pressure|memory pressure|pid pressure|network unavailable"), "node_pressure_conditions",
    true(), "scheduler_message_other")
| eval severity=case(
    pending_soak_min>=25 AND match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient|taint|affinity|topology|gpu|0/\\d+ nodes|pressure"), "critical",
    pending_soak_min>=20 AND len(fs_message)>30, "critical",
    pending_soak_min>=15 AND len(fs_message)>20, "high",
    pending_soak_min>=8 AND match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)0/\\d+ nodes|insufficient|taint|affinity|topology|didn\'t match"), "high",
    pending_soak_min>=8 AND scheduler_unschedulable_pods>=1, "medium",
    pending_soak_min>=8, "medium",
    true(), "low")
| where severity IN ("critical","high","medium") AND (pending_soak_min>=8 OR len(fs_message)>40)
| appendcols [| tstats summariesonly=t avg(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS cim_mem_fleet_avg FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance.Memory earliest=-2h@h latest=now | head 1 ]
| table cluster namespace pod pending_soak_min cluster_peak_pending scheduler_pending_pods scheduler_unschedulable_pods scheduler_schedule_attempts_total failure_class fs_message workload_tier owner_team severity cim_mem_fleet_avg fs_event_time sched_lane
| sort - severity +cluster +namespace +pod
description: Detects Kubernetes Pods stuck in phase Pending because kube-scheduler cannot place them: kube_pod_status_phase remains pending beyond a soak window, kube_pod_status_scheduled reports condition false, v1.Events carry reason FailedScheduling with fit narratives such as zero nodes available summaries, insufficient cpu, memory, ephemeral-storage, or pod count, untolerated taints, node or pod affinity mismatches, unsatisfiable topology spread constraints, node pressure predicates, local volume node affinity conflicts, and extended resource exhaustion including GPU. Cluster-level scheduler gauges scheduler_pending_pods and scheduler_unschedulable_pods add fleet context. The monitoring axis is scheduler fit and placement, not unbound PVC provisioning (UC-3.2.5), not cluster autoscaler cloud scale-out refusal (UC-3.2.46), not namespace ResourceQuota admission blocks (UC-3.2.4), and not CrashLoopBackOff after assignment (UC-3.2.10).
value: Cuts mean time to repair when releases stall without crash evidence: responders see cluster, namespace, pod, soak minutes, fused FailedScheduling text, classified failure_class, scheduler backlog gauges, governance tier, owning team, and optional fleet memory context in one row instead of correlating kubectl describe, metrics consoles, and scheduler logs by hand. Customer-visible reliability improves when capacity, affinity, toleration, and topology mistakes are fixed before SLO burn accumulates.
implementation: Provision k8s_metrics and k8s (or k8s_events) with distinct HEC tokens; scrape kube-state-metrics for kube_pod_status_phase and kube_pod_status_scheduled and kube-scheduler gauges when policy allows; stream kube:events; publish lookups/critical_namespaces.csv; save uc_3_2_2_scheduler_failed_scheduling every five minutes with earliest=-45m@m latest=now; page gold namespaces when pending_soak_min crosses eight minutes with FailedScheduling text or elevated scheduler_unschedulable_pods; validate using a lab Deployment that over-requests CPU against a single-node pool.
visualization: Primary table mirroring the closing SPL projection; secondary single value for critical severity row count; timechart of pending_soak_min by namespace; drilldown to kube:events for the same pod.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t latest(Application_State.state) AS app_state latest(Application_State.info) AS app_info FROM datamodel=Application_State WHERE nodename=Application_State (Application_State.app="kubernetes" OR Application_State.app="k8s" OR like(Application_State.app, "%kube%")) earliest=-4h@h latest=@h BY Application_State.dest
| rename Application_State.dest AS cim_host
| join type=left max=0 cim_host [| tstats summariesonly=t avg(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS cpu_load_pct FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance.CPU earliest=-4h@h latest=@h BY Performance.host | rename Performance.host AS cim_host ]
| table cim_host app_state app_info cpu_load_pct
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Splunk CIM Application_State and Performance summaries on entities that represent Kubernetes nodes or worker hosts so tstats overlays stay warm when correlating scheduling failures with underlying saturation; retain at least thirty days for quarterly capacity reviews.

references.title: Google Cloud GKE troubleshooting pod scheduling
Amazon EKS best practices guide — scheduling

knownFalsePositives: Pods may wait on purpose while new node pools come online during planned capacity expansion; require soak minutes and explicit FailedScheduling text before paging. Development clusters that intentionally run hot will show insufficient resource messages during load tests; dampen via workload_tier sandbox. Brief Pending windows during cluster autoscaler upscale can occur until nodes register and become Ready; correlate UC-3.2.46 before treating as pure scheduler misconfiguration. Single-zone topology spread on tiny clusters often fails until architects relax constraints; treat as education not outage when sandbox namespaces experiment. GPU Pods may wait until GPU nodes finish provisioning; pair with node pool timelines. Taint-based maintenance windows during approved changes should carry time-boxed suppressions. Pods using nodeSelector for spot-instance pools may wait when pools drain; FinOps and reliability should agree on acceptable wait. Headless StatefulSet Pods tied to local PersistentVolumes may wait for hostname alignment; distinguish from generic CPU starvation using volume affinity language in events. Rolling upgrades can transiently raise scheduler_pending_pods; compare to change records. Duplicate kube-state-metrics scrapes without deduplication can distort soak until relabel rules converge. Test namespaces that pin impossible affinities will always fire; exclude via lookup flags.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — episodes when gold namespaces show critical scheduling backlog alongside service KPI burn

detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control together with Kubernetes platform engineering, scheduling special interest groups, capacity management, and observability teams who operate Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector fleets across cloud and on-premises footprints. UC-3.2.2 is the kube-scheduler placement and fit failure plane: it answers whether Pods remain in phase Pending because the default scheduler could not find a suitable node, surfacing FailedScheduling events, kube-state-metrics signals kube_pod_status_phase for phase Pending with soak timing, kube_pod_status_scheduled with condition false, and optional kube-scheduler Prometheus gauges such as scheduler_pending_pods, scheduler_unschedulable_pods, and scheduler_schedule_attempts_total scraped from the scheduler metrics endpoint. UC-3.2.5 isolates PersistentVolumeClaim binding and CSI provisioning failures where storage objects never become Ready; a Pod can be Pending for volume reasons without a rich FailedScheduling message, so route volume-specific evidence there. UC-3.2.46 isolates Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter refusal to add nodes when cloud quotas, max node group sizes, or launch errors block scale-out; this UC still fires when autoscaler succeeds but the scheduler cannot fit Pods because of taints, affinity, topology spread, or resource starvation on existing nodes. UC-3.2.4 isolates namespace ResourceQuota admission exhaustion that rejects creates before scheduling completes; pair alerts when Splunk shows FailedCreate exceeded quota in the same namespace minute. UC-3.2.10 documents CrashLoopBackOff after placement; scheduling must succeed before crash semantics matter.

Index and token contracts precede saved-search authoring. Stand up index=k8s_metrics for prometheus:scrape:metrics text or normalized metric events scraped from kube-state-metrics Deployments and from kube-scheduler or kube-prometheus-stack ServiceMonitor endpoints where security approves scraping the control plane component. Stand up index=k8s or index=k8s_events for sourcetype=kube:events streams with consistent cluster labels matching your kube-state-metrics external labels. Issue distinct HEC tokens per index class with role-based search restrictions so developers can troubleshoot their namespaces without reading kube-system audit bodies. Document retention: metrics hot buckets at least fourteen days for replay, events at least thirty days for scheduling timelines.

Deploy kube-state-metrics with pod metric families enabled so kube_pod_status_phase exposes labels namespace, pod, uid, and phase, and kube_pod_status_scheduled exposes condition labels per upstream documentation. Scrape intervals of thirty seconds are typical; fifteen seconds improves soak fidelity at license cost. For managed Kubernetes, confirm whether the control plane exposes scheduler metrics to your tenant scrape targets or whether you must run an in-cluster proxy; Amazon EKS, Google GKE, and Microsoft AKS differ on how kube-scheduler metrics surface to user workloads.

Governance lookup lookups/critical_namespaces.csv must contain namespace, workload_tier taking values prod, production, preprod, staging, dev, sandbox, and owner_team or on_call_team strings for routing gold namespaces to voice bridges. Refresh weekly from GitOps exports or service catalog pipelines and attach commit hashes when auditors ask for lineage. Gold rows route critical severity to immediate paging when FailedScheduling messages name insufficient CPU, memory, extended resources, or unsatisfiable topology spread alongside long soak minutes. Silver rows open tickets with platform scheduling carbon copy. Bronze and sandbox rows remain dashboard-only unless an executive override marks customer impact.

Risk briefing: a Pod stuck Pending blocks Deployments, StatefulSets, Jobs, and operator-managed workloads from becoming Ready. The scheduler emits concise English reasons in events; Splunk preserves them longer than default etcd TTL in many clusters. Silently dropping pod labels at scrape time makes the control blind while kubectl still shows Pending Pods. Node pressure conditions and taints are normal during image pulls and upgrades; the soak gate reduces paging on sub-minute placement retries.

Licensing note: multisearch arms and appendcols tstats overlays keep search cost predictable; avoid unbounded joins across full metric indexes without filters. Privacy note: event messages can include workload codenames; restrict dashboard ACLs.

Differentiation recap: kube-scheduler fit and placement failures with FailedScheduling narrative and scheduler gauges, not PVC provisioning, not autoscaler cloud refusal, not namespace quota admission, not post-placement crash loops.

Collector architecture notes: self-managed clusters scrape kube-state-metrics inside the mesh; air-gapped environments mirror images before enabling scheduler metric scrapes. Dual-stack IPv6 clusters must preserve consistent instance labels in relabel configs.

FinOps alignment: insufficient CPU and memory messages precede expensive scale-out decisions; pair with capacity reviews before blindly raising node counts.

Reliability alignment: single-replica Deployments may stall entirely when the only Pod cannot schedule; critical severity should open a bridge, not a ticket-only workflow.

Security alignment: scheduler metrics endpoints require RBAC; store bearer tokens in vaults with quarterly rotation.

Performance alignment: keep multisearch arms narrow with explicit metric names to prevent accidental high-cardinality explosions from unrelated kube-state-metrics families.

Training alignment: teach responders the difference between Pending caused by scheduling versus Pending caused by containers not starting after assignment.

Documentation alignment: wiki-map this UC beside kube-scheduler, scheduling framework, and cluster troubleshooting documentation.

Review cadence: quarterly replay one historical scheduling outage through the SPL after kube-state-metrics upgrades.

Escalation alignment: critical plus gold workload_tier engages platform and application leadership jointly.

Telemetry hygiene: deduplicate overlapping Prometheus agents that scrape kube-state-metrics without honor_labels discipline.

Governance alignment: CAB records should cover HEC token ACL changes and ServiceMonitor edits together when scrape paths move.


Platform scheduling note 1: watch for topology spread on single-zone lab clusters; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 2: watch for GPU extended resources and device plugin labels; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 3: watch for local PersistentVolumes and hostname pinning; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 4: watch for Pod priority and preemption interactions; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 5: watch for static CPU manager and topology manager constraints; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 6: watch for Windows node pool label differences; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 7: watch for control plane upgrades and transient scheduler_pending_pods spikes; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 8: watch for etcd latency stretching scheduling attempt duration; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 9: watch for admission webhook mutations after kubectl apply; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 10: watch for service mesh sidecars inflating resource requests; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 11: watch for HPA scale-out creating new Pods that must schedule; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 12: watch for new nodes with taints requiring tolerations after scale-out; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 13: watch for Karpenter instance family mismatches; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 14: watch for spot interruption storms versus scheduler fit; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 15: watch for allocatable versus capacity and overcommit ratios; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 16: watch for maxPods per node and cloud ENI ceilings; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 17: watch for ImagePullBackOff versus scheduler Pending; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 18: watch for init container resource sums in fit math; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 19: watch for dynamic resource allocation API evolution; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Platform scheduling note 20: watch for air-gapped clusters without scheduler scrape access; extend coalesce ladders after migrations and document compensating kubectl procedures when metrics are unavailable.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Deploy a ServiceMonitor or PodMonitor that selects kube-state-metrics and scrapes port 8080 metrics path /metrics with intervals and timeouts aligned to your kube-prometheus-stack baseline. Relabel rules must preserve namespace, pod, uid, phase, and condition labels emitted by your kube-state-metrics version so Splunk coalesce ladders stay short.

Add a second scrape job for kube-scheduler metrics when your RBAC and network policy allow reaching the scheduler secure port or the metrics proxy Service published by your distribution. OpenTelemetry Collector prometheus receiver jobs should forward scheduler series into the same k8s_metrics index with sourcetype=prometheus:scrape:metrics and identical cluster tags. Pair metrics with a kubernetes_events or k8s_events receiver in watch mode so FailedScheduling narratives arrive with the same cluster field your metrics carry.

Example ServiceMonitor skeleton for kube-state-metrics:

```yaml
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: kube-state-metrics-pods
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    release: prom
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app.kubernetes.io/name: kube-state-metrics
  namespaceSelector:
    matchNames:
      - kube-system
  endpoints:
    - port: http-metrics
      interval: 30s
      path: /metrics
```

Collector fragment showing events export:

```yaml
receivers:
  k8s_events:
    auth_type: serviceAccount
    namespaces: []
exporters:
  splunk_hec/events:
    token: ${SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN_K8S_EVENTS}
    endpoint: https://splunk-hec.example:8088/services/collector/event
    index: k8s
    sourcetype: kube:events
service:
  pipelines:
    logs/events:
      receivers: [k8s_events]
      exporters: [splunk_hec/events]
```

Sample critical_namespaces.csv:

```csv
namespace,workload_tier,owner_team,criticality
prod-checkout,prod,checkout-sre,gold
sandbox-batch,sandbox,data-platform,bronze
```

props and transforms guidance: normalize __name__, value, namespace, pod, phase, condition, and cluster labels onto indexed fields; keep camelCase aliases from OTEL exporters in transforms.conf so SPL coalesce lists stay stable.

Validation before alert authoring: index=k8s_metrics kube_pod_status_phase earliest=-30m must return phase pending with non-zero values in busy clusters; index=k8s sourcetype=kube:events reason=FailedScheduling earliest=-24h should return rows when workloads are misconfigured. Clock skew between API server, kube-state-metrics, and Splunk _time must stay under sixty seconds.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as saved search uc_3_2_2_scheduler_failed_scheduling with five-minute schedule, dispatch earliest=-45m@m, dispatch latest=now, and throttle duplicate rows per cluster namespace pod for ninety minutes unless severity escalates from medium to critical. Route critical to immediate voice paging with platform scheduling bridges; route high to high-priority tickets with SMS backup for gold namespaces from critical_namespaces.csv.

Pipeline understanding for operators: the opening comment macro lists indexes, soak threshold, and time horizon. multisearch fans kube_pod_status_phase pending timelines, kube_pod_status_scheduled condition false snapshots, and kube:events FailedScheduling arms so a silent failure in one path still surfaces others. streamstats measures continuous Pending duration per Pod using one-minute buckets. coalesce ladders normalize cluster, namespace, and pod labels across EKS, GKE, and AKS field habits. The cluster-level join pulls scheduler_pending_pods, scheduler_unschedulable_pods, and scheduler_schedule_attempts_total for situational awareness. inputlookup critical_namespaces.csv enriches workload_tier and owner_team. appendcols runs an accelerated tstats average against Performance.Memory as a fleet memory pressure hint when Common Information Model summaries exist. eventstats adds cluster_peak_pending. case maps failure_class from canonical scheduler message patterns and assigns severity using soak minutes, message content, and unschedulable gauges. The closing table lists fourteen analyst columns.

Paste-and-run SPL for alerts and dashboards must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-3.2.2 K8s kube-scheduler FailedScheduling axis. idx=k8s_metrics,k8s earliest=-45m@m latest=now pending_soak_alert_min=8")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, aks_cluster_name, resource_cluster, ""))))
      | eval mn=trim(toString(coalesce(__name__, metric_name, name, "")))
      | eval mv=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(value, metric_value, Value, "")), 10)
      | eval ph=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(phase, phase_name, ""))))
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(coalesce(namespace, k8s_namespace, kube_namespace, ns, "")))
      | eval pod=trim(toString(coalesce(pod, pod_name, "")))
      | where mn="kube_pod_status_phase" AND ph="pending" AND mv>0 AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(namespace)>0 AND len(pod)>0
      | bucket _time span=1m
      | stats max(mv) AS pend BY cluster namespace pod _time
      | where pend>0
      | sort 0 cluster namespace pod + _time
      | streamstats current=t global=f first(_time) AS pend_start BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval pending_soak_min=round((now()-pend_start)/60, 3)
      | stats latest(pending_soak_min) AS pending_soak_min BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval sched_lane="phase_pending"
      | eval fs_message=""
      | eval fs_event_time=0 ]
    [ search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, aks_cluster_name, resource_cluster, ""))))
      | eval mn=trim(toString(coalesce(__name__, metric_name, name, "")))
      | eval mv=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(value, metric_value, Value, "")), 10)
      | eval cond=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(condition, ""))))
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(coalesce(namespace, k8s_namespace, kube_namespace, ns, "")))
      | eval pod=trim(toString(coalesce(pod, pod_name, "")))
      | where mn="kube_pod_status_scheduled" AND cond="false" AND mv>0 AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(namespace)>0 AND len(pod)>0
      | stats latest(mv) AS unsched_metric BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval pending_soak_min=0
      | eval sched_lane="scheduled_false"
      | eval fs_message=""
      | eval fs_event_time=0 ]
    [ search (index=k8s OR index=k8s_events) sourcetype="kube:events" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, source_cluster, ""))))
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(coalesce(metadata_namespace, namespace, involvedObject_namespace, "")))
      | eval pod=trim(toString(coalesce(involvedObject_name, `involvedObject.name`, "")))
      | eval ik=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(involvedObject_kind, `involvedObject.kind`, ""))))
      | eval reason=trim(toString(coalesce(reason, Reason, "")))
      | eval msg=toString(coalesce(message, Message, ""))
      | where reason="FailedScheduling" AND ik="pod" AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(namespace)>0 AND len(pod)>0
      | stats latest(msg) AS fs_message max(_time) AS fs_event_time BY cluster namespace pod
      | eval pending_soak_min=0
      | eval sched_lane="kube_event" ]
| stats max(pending_soak_min) AS pending_soak_min values(sched_lane) AS lane_mv max(fs_event_time) AS fs_event_time values(fs_message) AS fs_msg_mv BY cluster namespace pod
| eval fs_message=coalesce(nullif(trim(mvindex(mvdedup(fs_msg_mv),0)),""), "")
| eval sched_lane=mvjoin(mvdedup(lane_mv), "|")
| eventstats max(pending_soak_min) AS cluster_peak_pending BY cluster
| join type=left max=0 cluster
    [| search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-45m@m latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name, cluster_name, cluster, eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, aks_cluster_name, resource_cluster, ""))))
      | eval mn=trim(toString(coalesce(__name__, metric_name, name, "")))
      | eval mv=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(value, metric_value, Value, "")), 10)
      | where mn IN ("scheduler_pending_pods","scheduler_unschedulable_pods","scheduler_schedule_attempts_total")
      | eval sp=if(mn=="scheduler_pending_pods",mv,null())
      | eval su=if(mn=="scheduler_unschedulable_pods",mv,null())
      | eval sat=if(mn=="scheduler_schedule_attempts_total",mv,null())
      | stats latest(sp) AS scheduler_pending_pods latest(su) AS scheduler_unschedulable_pods latest(sat) AS scheduler_schedule_attempts_total BY cluster ]
| join type=left max=0 namespace
    [| inputlookup critical_namespaces.csv
      | eval namespace=trim(toString(namespace))
      | eval workload_tier=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(workload_tier, env_tier, tier, criticality, "standard"))))
      | eval owner_team=trim(toString(coalesce(owner_team, squad, on_call_team, "")))
      | fields namespace workload_tier owner_team ]
| fillnull value=0 pending_soak_min cluster_peak_pending scheduler_pending_pods scheduler_unschedulable_pods scheduler_schedule_attempts_total fs_event_time
| fillnull value="" fs_message sched_lane
| eval failure_class=case(
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient cpu"), "insufficient_cpu",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient memory"), "insufficient_memory",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient ephemeral-storage|insufficient ephemeral storage"), "insufficient_ephemeral_storage",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient.*pods|too many pods|exceeded maxpod"), "pod_count_exhaustion",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)didn\'t match pod\'s node affinity|didn\'t match node selector|node(s)? didn\'t match pod\'s node affinity"), "node_affinity_or_selector",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)had untolerated taint|not tolerate|untolerated taint"), "taints_without_tolerations",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)topology spread constraint|topologyspreadconstraints"), "topology_spread_unsatisfiable",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)0/\\d+ nodes are available"), "no_nodes_available_summary",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)match pod affinity|match inter-pod affinity|pod affinity"), "pod_affinity_anti_affinity",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)nvidia\\.com/gpu|amd\\.com/gpu|gpu"), "extended_resource_gpu",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)node(s)? had volume node affinity conflict|required node missing|local persistent volume"), "local_pv_or_volume_node_affinity",
    match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)node(s)? had disk pressure|memory pressure|pid pressure|network unavailable"), "node_pressure_conditions",
    true(), "scheduler_message_other")
| eval severity=case(
    pending_soak_min>=25 AND match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)insufficient|taint|affinity|topology|gpu|0/\\d+ nodes|pressure"), "critical",
    pending_soak_min>=20 AND len(fs_message)>30, "critical",
    pending_soak_min>=15 AND len(fs_message)>20, "high",
    pending_soak_min>=8 AND match(lower(fs_message), "(?i)0/\\d+ nodes|insufficient|taint|affinity|topology|didn\'t match"), "high",
    pending_soak_min>=8 AND scheduler_unschedulable_pods>=1, "medium",
    pending_soak_min>=8, "medium",
    true(), "low")
| where severity IN ("critical","high","medium") AND (pending_soak_min>=8 OR len(fs_message)>40)
| appendcols [| tstats summariesonly=t avg(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS cim_mem_fleet_avg FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance.Memory earliest=-2h@h latest=now | head 1 ]
| table cluster namespace pod pending_soak_min cluster_peak_pending scheduler_pending_pods scheduler_unschedulable_pods scheduler_schedule_attempts_total failure_class fs_message workload_tier owner_team severity cim_mem_fleet_avg fs_event_time sched_lane
| sort - severity +cluster +namespace +pod
```

Alert actions: include cluster, namespace, pod, pending_soak_min, failure_class, fs_message, severity, scheduler_unschedulable_pods, and owner_team in tickets; link to Dashboard Studio Kubernetes scheduling health view; attach three redacted kube events when legal approves.

Operational notes: when kube_pod_status_scheduled samples are missing because a scrape dropped labels, investigate relabel configs before muting. When only events fire without Pending metrics, assume kube-state-metrics outage rather than healthy scheduling.

Dashboard publishing: heatmap of pending_soak_min by namespace; single value critical count; table sorted by severity then tier.

Performance tuning: if Job Inspector shows multisearch queueing, materialize five-minute rollups into a summary index and point this alert at summaries while retaining raw scrapes for investigations.

Step 4 — Validate

Synthetic insufficient CPU: in namespace qa-sched-lab apply a Deployment whose Pod requests exceed any single node allocatable in that pool, confirm kube:events shows FailedScheduling with insufficient cpu language, kube_pod_status_phase stays pending beyond eight minutes, and uc_3_2_2_scheduler_failed_scheduling returns a qualifying row.

Synthetic taint mismatch: taint a small lab node group with NoSchedule key dedicated=test value true effect NoSchedule, deploy a Pod without tolerations that targets that pool via nodeSelector, confirm messages reference untolerated taint, then remove the taint and verify recovery.

Synthetic affinity mismatch: apply a requiredDuringScheduling node affinity for a label no node carries, confirm FailedScheduling references node affinity, fix labels or affinity, confirm the alert clears.

Field sanity: rename a sandbox forwarder label to pod_name only and verify coalesce still resolves pod. RBAC: readers without index access must see zero rows.

Correlation: compare alert timestamps to kubectl describe pod events and kubectl get events --field-selector reason=FailedScheduling; misalignment often means metrics lagged behind API writes.

Extended validation: snapshot the alert table before and after fixing requests and limits; store kubectl get events output; verify streamstats soak resets after the Pod schedules or deletes.

Investigative clones: widen earliest to four hours only in ad-hoc searches to reconstruct intermittent placement failures during rolling upgrades; keep the saved alert window at forty-five minutes for cost control.

Audit correlation: when legal requests evidence, export the closing table with redacted fs_message bodies while preserving failure_class and timestamps for accountability.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case 1 — Insufficient CPU or memory on existing nodes: raise requests modestly, add nodes via capacity change, or reduce competing workloads; confirm allocatable versus requests in node describe output.

Case 2 — Insufficient ephemeral storage requests: clean image layers on nodes, expand disk, or lower ephemeral requests; pair with node disk pressure metrics.

Case 3 — Pod count per node exhausted: raise maxPods where supported, add nodes, or reduce per-node Pod density; validate CNI limits with network platform.

Case 4 — Required node affinity or nodeSelector mismatch: fix labels on nodes or relax selectors in Git; re-run rollout.

Case 5 — Untolerated taints including maintenance NoSchedule and NoExecute: add matching tolerations only when policy allows, or remove taints after maintenance completes.

Case 6 — Inter-pod affinity or anti-affinity cannot be satisfied: widen topology keys, reduce replicas temporarily, or split Deployments across namespaces with guidance from application architects.

Case 7 — Topology spread constraints unsatisfiable in small or single-zone clusters: relax maxSkew, add zones, or shrink replica counts during drills.

Case 8 — Extended resource exhaustion such as GPU: provision GPU node pools, lower GPU requests, or enable time-slicing where approved; verify device plugin health.

Case 9 — Local PersistentVolume node affinity conflicts: ensure Pod nodeSelector matches the volume hostname binding or migrate volumes under change control.

Case 10 — Node pressure conditions block placement: resolve disk, memory, PID, or network pressure on candidates; cordon and drain unhealthy nodes when safe.

Case 11 — Symptoms resemble this UC but PVCs never bind: hand off to UC-3.2.5 when events reference volume provisioning rather than fit summaries.

Case 12 — Symptoms resemble this UC but namespace quota denies creates: hand off to UC-3.2.4 when admission messages reference exceeded quota rather than zero nodes available.

Closing checklist: five step headers use plain em dashes as mandated; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl JSON field; multisearch covers pending phase, scheduled false, and FailedScheduling events; streamstats implements soak timing; join pulls scheduler gauges; inputlookup enriches governance; case implements severity tiers; appendcols provides tstats Performance overlay; final table includes fourteen columns; monitoringType lists Reliability and Availability; cimModels lists Application_State and Performance; equipment lists kubernetes and opentelemetry; equipmentModels lists kubernetes_k8s.

Runbook micro-sections: if severity is critical and failure_class is insufficient_cpu, attach utilization charts before approving hardware buys. If failure_class is taints_without_tolerations, link change records for maintenance windows. If cluster_peak_pending is high but this Pod is low priority, review PriorityClass policy before eviction decisions.

FinOps alignment: chart scheduler_schedule_attempts_total trends only after confirming counter semantics on your cluster version to avoid misreading cumulative counters during investigations.

Reliability alignment: pair with UC-3.2.46 when FailedScheduling persists only seconds after scale-out events to separate cloud denial from pure fit failure.

Security alignment: redact internal hostnames in tickets when fs_message includes node names tied to regulated data halls.

Performance alignment: summary index materializations should retain cluster namespace pod keys to preserve deduplication after alert throttles.

Governance alignment: weekly CSV exports of qualifying rows with lookup commit hashes satisfy internal platform audit requests without exposing raw metrics dumps.

Training alignment: game-day inject impossible affinity in sandbox monthly so new responders recognize failure_class taxonomy quickly.

Documentation alignment: link this saved search URL from the Kubernetes scheduling runbook index page maintained by platform engineering.

Closing narrative: this UC closes the gap between generic Pending noise and actionable scheduler fit evidence by demanding FailedScheduling text, kube-state-metrics phase and scheduled condition signals, soak timing, scheduler gauges, and ownership metadata in one row so incident commanders spend seconds deciding among capacity, placement policy, and maintenance hypotheses.
grandmaExplanation: We watch when new instances cannot be placed on any machine because the scheduler keeps reporting that nothing fits, and we show how long they waited and why. That helps teams fix capacity, placement rules, or maintenance settings before customers feel missing service.
hardware: Applies to Amazon EKS, Google GKE, Microsoft AKS, Red Hat OpenShift, VMware Tanzu, and self-managed Kubernetes where kube-state-metrics exposes pod scheduling metrics and kube:events reach Splunk; x86_64 and Arm64 node fleets are in scope when label contracts remain consistent.
